1: Another oracle-reception formula opening a distinct unit. Chapter 22 is a sustained judicial proceeding — YHWH as prosecuting attorney presenting the case against Jerusalem in legal form (hatishpot, 'will you judge?' — the interrogative implies Ezekiel is being commissioned as a judge). The catalogue of specific offenses (vv. 6-12) is the most comprehensive indictment list in Ezekiel.
2: ha'ir haddamim ('the city of blood'): a compound construct — Jerusalem named by its defining characteristic. Damim (bloods, plural) suggests multiple acts of bloodshed, accumulated guilt. The city's identity is fixed by what it has done with blood — the violation of Genesis 9:6 and Leviticus 17:11 ('the blood is the life') on a systemic scale. Hoda'at et kol-to'evoteiha ('make known all her abominations'): the prophet as prosecutorial witness.
3: ir shofechet dam betocha ('a city that sheds blood within herself'): the blood is internal — Jerusalem's bloodguilt is not from external violence against foreigners but from internal exploitation and injustice. Labo ittah ('to bring her time'): the blood-shedding hastens YHWH's judgment — each act of injustice moves the eschatological clock. The idols made within the city add ritual defilement to moral bloodguilt.
4: asha'mt bedam asher shafacht ('you have incurred guilt by the blood you shed'): asham (guilt/guilt offering) — the legal term for offense requiring restitution/atonement. The verb form here places the guilt as already incurred, not merely potential. Tameh biglulaich ('defiled by your idols'): tame' (ritually/morally unclean) — the same root used in Leviticus for the pollution that must be addressed through purification rituals, here applied to the systemic state of the city.
5: qerovah vesheloha mimecha tiqalasena ('those near and those far from you will mock you'): the universal mockery that falls on the humiliated city. Tamei hashem rabbat hamehumah ('defiled in name, abounding in turmoil'): name-defilement — the city whose name should mean 'city of peace' (Yerushalayim) is now defined by its defiled reputation. Turmoil (mehumah) as the defining atmosphere of the fallen city.
6: hinneh nesi'ei Yisrael ish lizro'o hayu vach ('the princes of Israel in you, each according to his arm/power, have shed blood'): ish lizro'o ('each by his arm') — each prince acting on his own strength/power, not under Torah constraint. The rulers are the first indicted in the catalogue. When leadership fails the covenant, every subsequent level of society follows. Hayu vach lema'an shefoch dam ('they were in you in order to shed blood') — the purpose-clause makes the bloodshed deliberate, not incidental.
7: av ve'em haqelu vach ('father and mother they have dishonored in you'): the first specific offense — violation of the fifth commandment (Ex 20:12; Deut 5:16). The catalogue that follows closely mirrors the Holiness Code (Lev 17-26) and the Decalogue: parent-honor, treatment of strangers, orphans, and widows. Lager asu ve'oshek bethochech ('the resident alien they have oppressed'): Leviticus 19:33-34 specifically commands fair treatment of the ger (resident alien).
8: miqdashi bazit ('my sanctuary you have despised'): the verb baz (to despise, treat with contempt) — active disregard for the sacred. Et-shabbatotai chillalt ('my sabbaths you have profaned'): challal (profane/desecrate) — the sabbath as covenant sign (Ex 31:13-17) is violated by treating it as an ordinary day. The pairing of sanctuary-contempt and sabbath-profanation brackets the whole cultic system of covenant order.
9: anshei rachil hayu vach lema'an shefoch dam ('slanderers were in you for the purpose of shedding blood'): rachil (tale-bearer/slanderer) — from the root meaning to go about/peddle; the one who circulates damaging reports. The legal prohibition in Lev 19:16 explicitly connects slanderous tale-bearing with endangering lives. Zimmah (lewdness/shameful conduct) — a strong term for sexual and moral perversion.
10: erva av gilla vach ('the nakedness of a father they have uncovered in you'): incest-language drawn directly from Leviticus 18 — 'uncovering nakedness' as the standard euphemism for prohibited sexual relations. The specific listing of violations — father's wife, women in their impurity — follows the Levitical list almost verbatim, demonstrating that the indictment is based on specific Torah legislation already in force.
11: ish et-eshet re'ehu asah to'evah ('each man did abomination with his neighbor's wife'): to'evah (abomination) — the term used throughout Leviticus 18 for the sexual violations that 'defile the land' (Lev 18:27). The list includes adultery, incest with daughter-in-law, and violation of a half-sister. The word to'evah links these sexual offenses to the idolatry described earlier — both are categorized as covenant-defiling abominations.
12: shochad lachu vach lema'an shefoch dam ('bribes they have taken in you for the purpose of shedding blood'): the economic/judicial corruption — bribery corrupts the justice system, leading to wrongful deaths. Neshech vetarbith laqachta ('interest and increase you have taken'): neshech (biting interest, from nashak, to bite) and tarbith (increase/profit) — the two prohibited forms of usury from Leviticus 25:36-37. Both exploit the economically vulnerable and violate the covenant economic order. Oti shachacht ('me you have forgotten') — the summary of all the offenses: the root cause is forgetting YHWH.
13: hinneh hikiti kapi el-bitzek'ach asher asit ('I have struck my hands together over your unjust gain'): YHWH performs the hand-clapping gesture (cf. 21:14, 17) — here as a sign of judgmental finality, possibly mimicking a legal sealing-of-verdict action. The gesture marks the decisive moment when the divine judge has heard enough evidence and pronounces the verdict.
14: hayya'amod libech ('will your heart endure?'): the interrogative challenges the city's imagined resilience. Im-techazaknah yadayich ('or will your hands be strong?'): the rhetorical questions expect 'no' — the heart that tolerated all the abominations will not endure when YHWH acts. Bayamim asher ani oseh otach ('in the days when I deal with you') — the divine dealing is certain; only the timing is unspecified.
15: vehifitzotich bagoyim vezereticha ba'aratsot ('I will scatter you among the nations and disperse you among the lands'): the covenant-curse of dispersion (Lev 26:33; Deut 28:64) explicitly enacted. Vatimachti timatchich mimmech ('and I will consume your filth from you'): the dispersion serves a purifying function — the removal of the impurity from the land, even through the harsh means of exile. The land vomits out its inhabitants (Lev 18:28).
16: venicchalit bach le'einei goyim ('you will be profaned in yourself before the eyes of the nations'): challal (profaned) — the city that profaned YHWH's sanctuary will itself be profaned before the watching nations. The public nature of the humiliation mirrors the public nature of the abominations: what was done visibly in the temple courtyards will be answered by visible public judgment. Yada'ta ki-ani YHWH: recognition-formula closing the indictment section.
17: Transition to the furnace/smelting allegory (vv. 17-22). The oracle now shifts from a legal catalogue of offenses to a metallurgical metaphor for the purifying action of judgment. This unit is complete in itself: diagnosis (vv. 17-19), process (vv. 20-21), and result (v. 22).
18: hayu-li beit Yisrael le-sigim ('the house of Israel has become dross to me'): sigim (dross, slag) — the worthless residue left after refining metal. All that Israel has become is listed: dross of silver, bronze, tin, iron, and lead. The precious metal (silver) has become slag; the base metals that contaminate it are now all that remains. The metallurgical vocabulary was familiar in the ancient Near East: Mesopotamian texts speak of gods refining their people as a smith refines silver.
19: lachen koh amar Adonai YHWH ya'an heyot kulchem le-sigim ('because all of you have become dross'): the lachen (therefore) marks the consequence that follows from the metallurgical diagnosis. The gathering of Israel into the furnace-city (kur) is explicitly compared to gathering metal for smelting: qibbetz etchem el-toch Yerushalayim ('I will gather you into the midst of Jerusalem').
20: kiqibbutz kesef unchoshet uvarzel ve'oferet ve'bdil el-toch kur ('as silver and copper and iron and lead and tin are gathered into a furnace'): the complete list of smelted metals gathered for the furnace. Lenafuach alav esh ('to blow fire upon it'): the bellows of the smelter that intensifies the heat. YHWH is both the metallurgist who has diagnosed the dross and the one who works the bellows: apen bi'api ushilchti etchem ('I will blow with my wrath and I will put you in and melt you').
21: vekanavasti etchem vefachati aleichem be'esh evrati venitu'sem betochatah ('I will gather you and blow upon you with the fire of my wrath, and you will be melted in its midst'): the extended metaphor reaches its climax — YHWH as the divine smelter working the furnace with his own breath of wrath. Evrati (my wrath/overflow) — the same word as 'outburst' — the uncontained energy that drives the smelting heat. The city is the furnace and the people are the metal to be melted.
22: kehintoch kesef betoch kur ken tinatchu betochah ('as silver is melted in the midst of a furnace, so you will be melted in its midst'): the simile made explicit — the metaphor's equation stated plainly. Veyeda'tem ki-ani YHWH shafachti chamati aleichem ('and you will know that I, YHWH, have poured out my fury upon you'): the recognition-formula closes the smelting oracle. The knowledge gained through the furnace-judgment is the goal of the whole process.
23: A new word-reception formula introduces the oracle against the land's classes (vv. 23-31). The land is diagnosed as lo ge'ushma beyom za'am ('not rained upon in the day of indignation') — a land that should have received the cleansing rain of YHWH's blessing has instead received only the drought of judgment. The social analysis that follows moves class by class through the failure of every sector of leadership.
24: lo ge'ushma beyom za'am ('not cleansed/rained upon in the day of wrath'): ge'ushma (a hapax legomenon, likely from gasham, to rain) — the land that has not received purifying rain is parched and unclean. The 'day of wrath' (yom za'am) echoes the 'day of YHWH' language of chapter 7. The land's condition as unwatered/uncleansed is the environmental correlate of its moral condition.
25: qesher nevi'eiha bequirbah ('a conspiracy of her prophets in her midst'): the prophets are indicted first — as the primary source of false guidance. Ke'aryeh sho'eg toreh taref ('like a roaring lion tearing the prey'): the prophets who should protect and guide the flock have become predators. Nefashot lachu ('they devour lives/souls') — the false prophets consume the people they claim to serve. Choshen nachalah lavku ('they have taken treasure and wealth') — the prophets profit materially from their falsehoods.
26: kohaneiha chamesu torati veyechalelu qodoshai ('her priests have done violence to my Torah and profaned my holy things'): chamas (violence/wrong) applied to Torah-treatment — the priests have violated the Torah they were commissioned to teach. Bein qodesh lechol lo hivdilu ('between holy and common they have not distinguished'): the priestly function is precisely the making of distinctions (Lev 10:10-11; Deut 33:10) — between holy and common, clean and unclean. The priests have blurred the very boundaries they exist to maintain. Shabbetotai he'elemu eineihem meyhem ('and from my Sabbaths they have hidden their eyes').
27: sareiha bequirbah ke'ze'evim torfei taref ('her officials in her midst are like wolves tearing the prey'): the officials/rulers are wolves — predators on the very community they govern. Lishpoch dam le'abed nefashot lema'an beroa batza' ('to shed blood, to destroy lives, for the sake of dishonest gain'): the officials' specific failure — they use their power not for justice but for extraction. The three classes — prophets (v25), priests (v26), and officials (v27) — are each indicted in order of expected covenant faithfulness.
28: venevi'eiha tachu lahem tafal ('and her prophets have plastered over them with whitewash'): the prophets' second indictment — they cover up the officials' crimes with false reassurance. Tafal (whitewash/plaster of no value) — cheap, cosmetic covering over structural defects. Chazim shav velohesim lahem kazav ('seeing false visions and divining lies for them'): the prophets serve the corrupt officials by providing theological cover — false visions that validate what the officials want to do. The same image as 13:10-16.
29: am ha'aretz asheq asheq vegazu gazel ('the people of the land have practiced oppression and committed robbery'): the final class — the common people themselves are indicted. Ani ve'evyon honu ('the poor and needy they have wronged'): hona (to oppress/wrong, often economically) — the most vulnerable members of society exploited by those who should protect them. The covenant's particular concern for the poor (Ex 22:21-27; Lev 19:9-10; Deut 24:10-22) is comprehensively violated at every social level.
30: avakesh me'ahem ish godar geder ve'omed baperetz lefanai ('and I sought from them a man building a wall and standing in the breach before me'): godar geder (one who builds a wall/fence) — the intercessor as structural restorer. Ve'omed baperetz ('standing in the breach'): the military image of a defender standing in the gap made in a city wall during siege — the last defender who could prevent the collapse. Moses was the archetypal breach-stander (Ps 106:23). YHWH searched for one human being among all the prophets, priests, officials, and people — and found none. Velo matza'ti: three words that carry the weight of Israel's total failure of intercessory representation.
31: vashpoch aleichem za'mi be'esh evrati killitihem ('I poured out my fury upon them; with the fire of my wrath I consumed them'): the result of finding no intercessor — judgment proceeds without any mediating figure to stay it. Derekham berosham natati ('their way I repaid on their own heads'): the covenant principle of lex talionis elevated to the national level — what Israel has done returns to Israel as its own judgment. The final word of the chapter is ne'um Adonai YHWH ('declares the Lord YHWH'), the sealing oracle-formula, affirming that the divine sentence is irrevocable.
